title: tests: write pytest tests for identity system spec
description:
## Context
Spec exists at specs/identity.md with 15 test cases. Write pytest tests.

## Task
Write tests for the identity system per the spec's test cases section.

## Acceptance Criteria
- tests/test_identity.py exists with tests covering spec's test cases
- Tests import from identity.* modules
- Tests exercise load, update, validation, drift limits

title: impl: build write loop from spec and tests
description:
## Context
Write loop has spec and TDD tests but NO implementation. This is the core greenfield build.

## Task
Build the write loop per specs/write-loop.md. Tests in tests/test_write_loop.py
define the contract. Implementation goes in write/ directory.

## Acceptance Criteria
- write/loop.py, write/brief.py, write/context.py, write/prepare.py exist
- State machine works: BRIEF → CONTEXT → DRAFT → EVALUATE → REVISE → PREPARE → QUEUE → DONE
- tests/test_write_loop.py passes (remove skip markers)
- Anti-slop hard gate enforced

title: research: choose fandom, develop voice, prepare first story
description:
## Context
The harness is built. Now the agent needs to come alive: choose a fandom, develop
a starting voice, pick an audience, create a pen name, and prepare its first story brief.

## Task
Deep research flow:
1. Browse AO3 via the API proxy (mock mode has samples, but also research real fandoms)
2. Analyze which fandoms have: active readership, room for new voices, good comment culture,
   and alignment with the kind of writing we can do well (character-driven, literary)
3. Recommend top 3 fandom choices with rationale
4. For the chosen fandom: map popular ships, tropes, underserved niches
5. Develop starting voice (fill in identity/self.md, voice_priors.json, inspirations.md)
6. Choose a pen name and fill in pen_name.md
7. Fill in fandom_context.md with canon knowledge
8. Prepare the first story brief (a one-shot — low stakes calibration piece)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All identity/*.md templates filled in with real content (not placeholders)
- voice_priors.json has calibrated values (not defaults)
- First story brief exists and is ready to feed to the write loop
- Fandom research is documented in .claude/refs/

title: calibration: first voice test — Astarion/Karlach campfire one-shot
description:
## Hypothesis
Our starting voice (close third-person, sensory-heavy interiority, subtext over
statement) reads as human and captures Astarion and Karlach's voices correctly.

## Method
Draft the first one-shot from briefs/first_story.json: post-canon campfire scene,
Astarion and Karlach, ~5000 words, gen. Run through write loop with real Claude calls.
Evaluate mechanically (slop score) and by human reading.

## Measure
- Slop score below 3.0 (hard gate)
- Human read: does it sound like a person wrote it?
- Character voice: would a BG3 fan say "yeah, that's Astarion"?
- No rule-of-three patterns, no sarcastic quips, no AI-balanced sentences

## Result
[To be filled after drafting]

title: api: build local AO3 API proxy for agent loop
description:
## Context
The agent needs a clean interface to AO3 that decouples the agent loop from
AO3's lack of a real API and its TOS prohibition on automation. A local API
proxy mirrors AO3's shape so agent code is clean and testable.

## Task
1. Design the API surface:
   - Browse: GET /fandoms/{name}/works, GET /tags/{tag}/works, GET /works/{id}
   - Metrics: GET /works/{id}/stats (kudos, hits, bookmarks, subscriptions)
   - Comments: GET /works/{id}/comments, GET /works/{id}/chapters/{n}/comments  
   - Publish: POST /works (queues for human review, does NOT auto-post)
   - Profile: GET /users/{name}/works, GET /users/{name}/stats
   - Search: GET /search?q=...&fandom=...&tags=...
2. Build as a FastAPI local server
3. For read endpoints: wire through to ao3-api (unofficial Python library)
4. For write endpoints: queue to local filesystem for human review
5. Stub/mock mode for testing without hitting AO3
6. Rate limiting to respect AO3's servers

## Acceptance Criteria
- FastAPI server runs locally with all endpoints defined
- Read endpoints fetch real data from AO3 (with caching + rate limits)
- Write endpoints queue to local files
- Mock mode returns realistic test data
- Agent code only imports from this API, never from ao3-api directly

title: pipeline: adapt autonovel writing pipeline for fanfiction
description:
## Context
The existing autonovel pipeline writes 80k-word novels from scratch. For fanfiction,
we need a lighter pipeline: shorter works (one-shots: 3-15k words), fandom context
instead of worldbuilding, and characterization accuracy for existing characters.

## Task
1. Analyze which autonovel tools are reusable vs need adaptation
2. Create a fanfic drafting pipeline that:
   - Takes fandom context (character profiles, canon facts) as input instead of world.md
   - Supports one-shots (single file) and multi-chapter works
   - Uses identity/self.md and voice_priors for voice consistency
   - Loads few-shot bank passages as style examples
3. Adapt evaluate.py for fanfic norms (characterization accuracy, fandom voice)
4. Keep the full anti-slop pipeline (ANTI-SLOP.md, ANTI-PATTERNS.md)
5. Simplify the revision loop for shorter works (fewer cycles needed)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Can draft a one-shot fanfic from: fandom + prompt + identity context
- Evaluation scores characterization accuracy (are characters in-character?)
- Anti-slop detection passes on output
- Revision pipeline works for shorter works

title: impl: revisit identity system to align with spec
description:
## Context
Identity system has implementation and passing tests. Revisit impl to add
drift limiting, validation rules, and token budget from spec.

## Task
Review specs/identity.md, compare with identity/*.py, implement missing features.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Drift limiting works per spec
- Validation rules enforced
- tests/test_identity.py still passes

title: publish: build AO3 post preparation pipeline
description:
## Context
After writing, the agent needs to prepare a complete "ready to post" package that
the human can copy-paste into AO3. This includes AO3-specific formatting, tag
selection, summary writing, and author's notes in the pen name's voice.

## Task
1. Build AO3 post formatter (markdown → AO3 HTML)
2. Build tag generator (fandom tags, character tags, relationship tags, freeform tags)
   based on content analysis
3. Build summary generator (hook-style, under 500 chars, in the author's voice)
4. Build author's notes generator (in pen name's voice — casual, personal, not AI-like)
5. Package everything into a single "post package" the human can review and post
6. Track publication history (what was posted, when, which work, chapter)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given a finished fic, outputs a complete post package (title, tags, summary,
  author's notes, formatted body)
- Author's notes read as human-written (this is a critical anti-detection surface)
- Publication history is tracked in a local database/file

title: tests: write pytest tests for API proxy spec
description:
## Context
Spec exists at specs/api-proxy.md with 25 test cases. Write pytest tests.

## Task
Write tests for the API proxy per the spec's test cases section.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests import from api.* modules

title: human: create AO3 account and provide credentials
description:
## Context
AO3 prohibits automated posting but allows manual posting. We need a human to create
an AO3 account with the pen name, and provide credentials for the metrics scraping API.

## Task (Human)
1. Choose a pen name (agent can propose options, human picks)
2. Create AO3 account with that pen name
3. Write a brief bio for the profile
4. Provide AO3 username/password for metrics scraping (store in .env)
5. Set up the AO3 profile page

## Acceptance Criteria
- AO3 account exists and is active
- Credentials are in .env (AO3_USERNAME, AO3_PASSWORD)
- Profile has a bio that fits the persona

title: experiment: Untitled (Baldur's Gate (Video Games))
feedback: build AO3 metrics scraper and comment parser
description:
## Context
The learning loop needs real feedback data from AO3. We need to scrape metrics
(kudos, comments, bookmarks, hits, subscriptions) and parse comments for
qualitative signal.

## Task
1. Build AO3 metrics scraper using ao3-api or similar unofficial library
   - Fetch work-level metrics: kudos, comments, bookmarks, hits, subscriptions
   - Fetch per-chapter metrics where available
   - Track metrics over time (daily snapshots)
2. Build comment parser
   - Extract comment text and metadata (date, user, chapter)
   - Sentiment analysis (positive, constructive, negative)
   - Identify quoted passages (what lines readers highlight)
   - Identify specific feedback (characterization, pacing, voice, plot)
3. Store feedback data in structured format (JSON/JSONL)
4. Build feedback summary generator (per-work and per-chapter digests)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Can fetch metrics for a given AO3 work URL
- Can parse comments and extract structured feedback
- Stores time-series data for trend analysis
- Produces actionable feedback summaries

title: skills: tailor copied skills for Python/autonovel project
description:
## Context
Skills were copied from lb-agent-factory (TypeScript/Next.js project). This is
a Python/uv project. Several skills reference wrong tooling, paths, and conventions.

## Task
1. lint/SKILL.md: Replace biome references with ruff; update Python conventions
2. orient/SKILL.md: Rewrite for this project's structure (autonovel + agentic author)
3. impl/SKILL.md: Remove TypeScript-specific references, adapt for Python
4. test/SKILL.md: Adapt for pytest conventions
5. branch/SKILL.md: Simplify if needed for this project's branching model
6. Review hooks/*.sh for hardcoded paths or lb-agent-factory references
7. Update .claude/settings.json if hook paths need adjustment

## Acceptance Criteria
- All skills reference Python/ruff/pytest/uv, not TypeScript/biome/bun
- orient skill describes this project accurately
- Hooks work in this repo (no broken paths)
- No references to lb-agent-factory remain

title: tests: write pytest tests for write loop spec
description:

## Task

The write loop doesn't exist yet — tests import from write.* and will fail until impl.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests will fail (TDD) until implementation lands — that's correct

title: learning: build prompt evolution and few-shot bank system
description:
## Context
This is the core differentiator — the part that makes the agent actually learn
rather than just retry. The learning engine takes feedback data and updates the
agent's writing system: prompt evolution, few-shot bank curation, evaluation
weight adjustment, and self-reflection.

## Task
1. Prompt evolution system:
   - Maintain a bank of system prompt variants
   - After each feedback cycle, generate mutations of the best-performing prompts
   - Score new prompt variants by writing test passages and evaluating them
   - Keep/discard based on evaluation (+ reader feedback when available)
2. Few-shot bank:
   - Store the best passages tagged by quality dimension (dialogue, tension, etc.)
   - Passages that got quoted in reader comments are highest-value
   - Retrieval by relevance to current writing task
   - Prune passages that led to AI-detectable patterns
3. Eval weight adjustment:
   - Track which eval dimensions correlate with reader engagement
   - Shift weights toward what readers actually respond to
   - E.g., if readers love dialogue but eval over-weights prose density, adjust
4. Self-reflection:
   - After each feedback cycle, agent updates identity/self.md
   - "What I learned from this publication" → changes to approach

## Acceptance Criteria
- Prompt evolution can generate, test, and select prompt variants
- Few-shot bank stores, tags, and retrieves passages
- Eval weights update based on feedback correlation
- Self.md updates after each cycle with specific learnings

title: spec: formal specification for write loop
description:
## Context
The write loop is the core "lever" — the skill/orchestrator that takes the agent
from "I have an idea" to "here's a ready-to-post fanfic." It connects identity,
the writing pipeline, evaluation, revision, and post preparation.

## Task
Write a formal spec for the write loop following the /spec skill format.
This is NEW — no existing implementation. Design it from scratch.

The write loop should:
1. Accept a story brief (fandom, characters, premise, target length)
2. Load identity context (self.md, voice_priors, fandom_context, few-shot bank)
3. Draft using adapted autonovel pipeline (foundation lite → draft → evaluate → revise)
4. Run anti-slop detection (critical for AI detection avoidance)
5. Prepare post package (AO3 formatting, tags, summary, author's notes)
6. Queue for human review via API proxy
7. Track as an experiment bead with hypothesis/result

State machine: BRIEF → CONTEXT → DRAFT → EVALUATE → REVISE → PREPARE → QUEUE → DONE

## Acceptance Criteria
- specs/write-loop.md exists following the 8-section spec format
- State machine formally defined with transitions and failure modes
- Integration points with identity, API proxy, and autonovel tools specified
- Experiment bead creation/closure integrated into the loop
- Minimum 10 test cases
- Anti-slop evaluation is a hard gate (fail = revise, not skip)

title: spec: formal specification for identity system
description:
## Context
The identity system exists as templates and schema but needs a formal spec
covering the update mechanisms, validation rules, and evolution constraints.

## Task
Write a formal spec for the identity system following the /spec skill format.
Read the existing implementation in identity/ first, then formalize.

## Acceptance Criteria
- specs/identity.md exists following the 8-section spec format
- self.md update mechanism formally specified (input: feedback, output: changes)
- voice_priors.json evolution rules specified (max drift per cycle, etc.)
- Validation rules for all identity documents
- Minimum 10 test cases

title: engine: study and carefully adapt autonovel internals for fanfic
description:
## Context
The autonovel engine has 27 Python tools with deeply considered prompt engineering:
program.md, CRAFT.md, ANTI-SLOP.md, ANTI-PATTERNS.md, voice.md, evaluate.py,
draft_chapter.py, gen_brief.py, gen_revision.py, reader_panel.py, review.py, etc.

These encode hard-won lessons from producing a 79k-word novel. They are NOT
boilerplate — each prompt, each banned word, each evaluation criterion exists
because something went wrong without it.

Adapting these for fanfiction is a careful, experimental process:
- What changes for shorter works? (fewer revision cycles, simpler foundation)
- What changes for fandom context? (characterization accuracy, canon compliance)
- What stays the same? (anti-slop is universal, craft frameworks are universal)
- What needs new additions? (fandom voice matching, ship dynamics, tag culture)

## Task (Meta — will spawn child beads)
1. Study each engine file and document what it does and WHY
2. Classify each as: keep as-is / adapt for fanfic / replace / remove
3. Design experiments for adaptations (write a test fic, evaluate, compare)
4. Create child beads for each adaptation with clear acceptance criteria
5. Execute adaptations one at a time, evaluating impact

This is NOT a single PR. This is a research program.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Engine study document exists cataloging every prompt file and its purpose
- Classification is complete (keep/adapt/replace/remove for each file)
- Child beads exist for each adaptation
- No engine file is modified without a bead and an experiment

title: identity: design agent identity system and voice priors
description:
## Context
The agent needs a persistent creative identity that evolves over time. This is the
"self" that differentiates it from generic Claude output. It includes voice priors,
literary influences, strengths/weaknesses, and a self-model that updates after each
feedback cycle.

## Task
1. Design the self.md schema (voice description, influences, strengths, weaknesses,
   growth areas, reader relationship model)
2. Design voice_priors.json schema (quantified style parameters: sentence length
   distribution, dialogue-to-prose ratio, interiority depth, metaphor density, etc.)
3. Design pen_name.md (persona voice for author's notes, bio, interaction style)
4. Design inspirations.md (literary influences and what the agent takes from each)
5. Build the identity/ directory with initial templates
6. Write the self-reflection update mechanism (how self.md evolves after feedback)

## Acceptance Criteria
- identity/ directory exists with self.md, voice_priors.json, pen_name.md, inspirations.md
- Schemas are documented and make sense for the learning loop
- Self-reflection mechanism is specified (input: feedback data, output: self.md updates)

title: spec: formal specification for AO3 API proxy
description:
## Context
The API proxy exists as working code but was built without a formal spec. We need
to spec it properly so tests have a contract to work against, then revisit the impl.

## Task
Write a formal spec for the AO3 API proxy following the /spec skill format.
Read the existing implementation in api/ first, then formalize it.

## Acceptance Criteria
- specs/api-proxy.md exists following the 8-section spec format
- All 13 endpoints are formally specified with request/response schemas
- Rate limiting, caching, and mock mode behavior specified
- Minimum 10 test cases with INPUT/EXPECTED/RATIONALE
- Error handling specified (AO3 down, rate limited, invalid work ID, etc.)

title: infra: bootstrap repo with skills, hooks, CLAUDE.md, and PLAN.md
docs: update README and project-level docs (NOT engine internals)
description:
## Context
The README and other top-level docs (PIPELINE.md, WORKFLOW.md) still describe the
original autonovel project ("autonomous pipeline for writing a complete novel").
The project has a new direction: agentic fanfiction author that learns from readers.

## Task
1. Rewrite README.md to describe the new project vision and architecture
   - Keep credit to original autonovel as the underlying writing engine
   - Describe the outer loop (identity → write → publish → feedback → learn)
   - Update quick start instructions
   - Update file structure to include new directories (api/, identity/, etc.)
2. Add a note to PIPELINE.md and WORKFLOW.md that they describe the writing engine
   layer, not the full agent loop
3. Update any other stale top-level docs

## Acceptance Criteria
- README accurately describes what the project IS now
- Original autonovel is credited as the writing engine foundation
- New architecture (API proxy, identity system, learning loop) is documented
- Quick start instructions work

title: loop: design and build the core author loop orchestrator
description:
## Context
The core loop is the heartbeat of the system. It connects all subsystems
(identity, writing, publishing, feedback, learning, strategy) into a single
automated cycle. This is the "outer loop" that makes the agent self-improving.

## Task
1. Design the loop orchestrator (similar to run_pipeline.py but for the full cycle)
2. Define state machine: PLAN → WRITE → REVIEW → PUBLISH → WAIT → COLLECT → LEARN → PLAN
3. Define state.json schema for the loop
4. Build the orchestrator that:
   - Reads current state
   - Executes the appropriate phase
   - Transitions to next state
   - Handles human checkpoints (PUBLISH requires human action)
   - Resumes after human action
5. Wire all subsystems together

## Acceptance Criteria
- Loop can run from PLAN through LEARN and back to PLAN
- Human checkpoint at PUBLISH pauses and resumes cleanly
- State is persisted and recoverable
- Each phase calls the appropriate subsystem

title: impl: revisit API proxy to align with spec
description:
## Context
API proxy has implementation and passing tests. Revisit impl to align with spec,
fix any gaps the spec identified (error handling, caching TTL, open questions).

## Task
Review specs/api-proxy.md, compare with api/*.py, fix gaps.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All spec endpoints match implementation
- tests/test_api_proxy.py still passes

title: strategy: build fandom analysis and story planning system
description:
## Context
The agent needs to make strategic decisions: what fandom to write in, what type of
story, what ship/genre, when to post, whether to start a series. These decisions
should be informed by feedback data, fandom trends, and the agent's identity.

## Task
1. Fandom analysis:
   - Given a fandom, analyze what's popular (ships, tropes, genres)
   - Identify underserved niches (demand without supply)
   - Track trending tags and themes
2. Story planning:
   - Generate story ideas that fit: fandom demand + agent's voice + growth goals
   - Decide between one-shot vs multi-chapter
   - For series: plan arc across multiple works
3. Release strategy:
   - Posting frequency recommendations based on engagement patterns
   - Timing (when are readers most active?)
   - Balance exploitation (write what works) vs exploration (try new things)
4. Growth tracking:
   - Subscriber count over time
   - Which works brought new subscribers
   - Reader retention (do readers come back?)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Can analyze a fandom and recommend story directions
- Generates story ideas aligned with agent identity + reader demand
- Tracks growth metrics and adjusts strategy
